headline: HDFC Bank, Axis Bank and more: 4 UPI credit cards that include features like cashbacks, rewards airport longue access
text: HDFC Bank, Axis Bank and more: 4 UPI credit cards that include features like cashbacks, rewards airport longue access. New UPI-enabled credit cards offer convenience and rewards. Kiwi, HDFC Bank, IDFC FIRST Bank, and Axis Bank provide options. Users can make payments via UPI apps and earn cashback, reward points, and other benefits. These cards combine UPI's ease with credit card advantages. Several banks and fintech companies have been offering UPI-enabled credit cards that allow users to make payments directly through UPI apps. Also, making payments may help them earn rewards and cashback. These cards offer the convenience of UPI and the benefits of a credit card, such as cashback, reward points, lounge access and other perks. Here are some popular UPI credit cards from Kiwi, HDFC Bank , IDFC FIRST Bank and Axis Bank . Also know the key benefits that they offer, as per data from Bankbazaar. Income Tax Guide Income Tax Union Budget FY 2026-27 Live Income Tax Slabs FY 2025-26 Income Tax Calculator 2025 Kiwi credit card features The card offers a flat 1.5% cashback on all UPI transactions. Instant cashback is credited to the wallet; this cashback can be withdrawn directly from the bank account. Offers 3 complimentary lounge visits with Kiwi Neonâs membership. The credit card is lifetime free. Also read: YES Bank credit card rules change from April 1: Relaxes threshold limits of fee waiver on select cards; revises rules for wallet loads, utility and toll payments HDFC Bank UPI RuPay credit card features Offers 3% CashPoints on groceries, supermarket, dining and PayZapp; 2% on utilities; and 1% on all other spends, including UPI (applicable on transactions above Rs 100). Each reward tier has a monthly cap of 500 CashPoints or equal to Rs 375 per month. Joining/annual fee: Rs 99, which is waived on annual spends of Rs 25,000 in the previous year. IDFC FIRST Bank HPCL Power+ credit card features Offers 2 reward points per Rs 150 spent on UPI retail spends. There is no cap on UPI rewards as unlimited rewards are allowed. One can enjoy welcome benefits worth Rs 2,250 on the card. The joining/renewal fee is Rs 199; The joining/renewal fee is reversed on annual spends of Rs 50,000 or more. Axis Bank SUPERMONEY RuPay credit card features Offers 3% rewards on UPI spends via super.money app. Helps save on refueling costs with 1% fuel surcharge waiver. The joining/renewal fee is nil. What is UPI? Unified Payments Interface (UPI) is a system that empowers users to add multiple bank and other allowed accounts (of any participating bank) into a UPI App, merging several banking features, seamless fund routing and merchant payments under one hood. Can a UPI RuPay credit card be used for UPI? According to the HDFC Bank website, âYes, RuPay credit cards can be seamlessly used for UPI transactions. Users can download and register on their preferred UPI app, such as PayZapp, GPay, PhonePe, Mobikwik, PayTM , BHIM or Slice. By registering the UPI RuPay Credit Card using the Credit Card PIN, users can make UPI payments and leverage the associated benefits, combining the strengths of credit cards on UPI platforms. Additionally, this card allows cash withdrawals at ATMs, providing users with a comprehensive financial tool for various transactions.â Which RuPay Credit Cards variants are available? RuPay Credit cards are available in the following variants: Classic Platinum Select How to apply for a RuPay credit card? To apply for a RuPay credit card, please contact one of HDFC Bankâs certified bank partners through any of its customer service channels such as branch, customer care portal or the bankâs credit card sourcing partner. How do I check the status of my RuPay credit card application? HDFC Bank says all you need is your application reference number and the mobile number to check it. The application reference number would have been sent as a text message (SMS) to the mobile number mentioned in the application form by the bank whoâs RuPay Credit Card has been applied. Kindly mention the same application reference number and the mobile number to check the status of the application with the Bank. ( Originally published on Mar 09, 2026 )

headline: SAT gives partial relief to Kotak AMC in Sebi case on fixed maturity plan payouts
text: SAT gives partial relief to Kotak AMC in Sebi case on fixed maturity plan payouts. The Securities Appellate Tribunal granted partial relief to Kotak Mahindra AMC in the fixed maturity plan case by setting aside Sebi’s fee disgorgement order but upheld monetary penalties. The ruling relates to delayed investor payouts linked to recoveries from underlying investments. Add as a Reliable and Trusted News Source Add Now! headline: RBI to start 3-day deliberations on interest rate from Wednesday
text: RBI to start 3-day deliberations on interest rate from Wednesday. The Reserve Bank's policy panel begins its meeting Wednesday to decide on interest rates. Experts expect a pause in rate cuts due to low inflation and steady growth. The decision, influenced by the Union Budget and a recent trade deal, will be announced Friday. This move aims to assess upcoming economic data and maintain stability. Add as a Reliable and Trusted News Source Add Now! (You can now subscribe to our Economic Times WhatsApp channel )

headline: Svatantra Microfin acquires Chaitanya India Fin; becomes 2nd largest NBFC-MFI
text: Svatantra Microfin acquires Chaitanya India Fin; becomes 2nd largest NBFC-MFI. Svatantra Microfin has successfully merged with Chaitanya India Fin Credit and Svatantra Holding, solidifying its position as India's second-largest NBFC-MFI. This strategic move, approved by the NCLT, creates a combined entity managing approximately Rs 22,000 crore in Assets Under Management. headline: Stock Market holidays in March 2026: NSE, BSE to remain shut on 3 days this month; check list
text: Stock Market holidays in March 2026: NSE, BSE to remain shut on 3 days this month; check list. NSE Holidays March 2026: Indian stock markets face three closures in March 2026. headline: OpenClaw unveils ClawHub Marketplace; draws more than 331,000 stars on GitHub
text: OpenClaw unveils ClawHub Marketplace; draws more than 331,000 stars on GitHub. Peter Steinberger, OpenClawâs creator, released the update. The platform allows users to deploy AI agents that can reason, respond, and execute tasks inside apps, such as Telegram and Discord, all while remaining fully self-hosted. The open-source artificial intelligence (AI) platform OpenClaw has introduced a plugin marketplace called ClawHub alongside a slate of new AI features aimed at accelerating the development of autonomous agents . The project, now boasting more than 331,000 stars on GitHub , is positioning itself as a central hub for next-generation âagenticâ computing, where AI systems can independently reason, act, and interact with tools. At the core of the release is ClawHub, a marketplace designed to let developers discover, install, and share plugins that extend agent capabilities. OpenClawâs creator, Peter Steinberger , released the update directly. The platform allows users to deploy AI agents that can reason, respond, and execute tasks inside apps such as Telegram and Discord, all while remaining fully self-hosted. Momentum is spilling into the broader AI ecosystem as Nvidia launched NemoClaw, an enterprise-grade security layer designed to harden OpenClaw deployments. Anthropic had earlier introduced Cowork Dispatch , a coordination framework for multi-agent workflows. Additionally, Mark Zuckerberg is reportedly developing an internal âCEO agentâ at Meta , inspired by OpenClawâs architecture and principles.

headline: If you want to have good governance, you cannot be in a yes-sir mode: Sashidhar Jagdishan
text: If you want to have good governance, you cannot be in a yes-sir mode: Sashidhar Jagdishan. We will convene multiple board meetings over the next month to get the views of all directors on decisions taken over time. We will re-examine them, evaluate the action points and see where we need to improve. This issue was not of our making; it was thrust upon us. We will take stock of past matters and address all issues-existing or new-without hiding anything, says Sashidhar Jagdishan, MD & CEO, HDFC Bank. headline: Indian lenders ask RBI to ease liquidity rules before policy meeting, sources say
text: Indian lenders ask RBI to ease liquidity rules before policy meeting, sources say. Indian banks are asking the Reserve Bank of India for regulatory changes. They face a deposit shortfall and rising bond yields. Lenders want a delay in liquidity coverage ratio norms. They also seek flexibility in shifting bonds and want cash reserve ratio to count as high-quality liquid assets. These requests aim to ease funding pressures. headline: Compensation for cyber fraud: RBIâs new initiative works for many customers but know when it doesnât work for others
text: Compensation for cyber fraud: RBIâs new initiative works for many customers but know when it doesnât work for others. Digital transactions are rising in India. This has led to an increase in digital frauds. The RBI is proposing compensation for customers losing money in small-value fraudulent transactions. This move aims to protect vulnerable users and build trust in the digital payment system. Banks and merchants will share some liability. The governmentâs post-pandemic digital push, along with improved payment ecosystem infrastructure, has resulted in a rise in consumption of digital channels in the country for transactions and payments. The Unified Payments Interface (UPI) as a mode for payments has become very popular, and the country has witnessed a significant uptick in volumes. Income Tax Guide Income Tax Union Budget FY 2026-27 Live Income Tax Slabs FY 2025-26 Income Tax Calculator 2025 However, the rise in volumes, ease of transacting, and popularity have also led to an increase in digital frauds, impacting customers at large. Innovative scams prompted the Reserve Bank of India (RBI) to continuously enhance awareness campaigns around incidents targeting customers. The RBI also instructed banks to put adequate safeguards in place to prevent such incidents. While the volume of digital fraud has been largely high, the values have been fairly smaller, given the nature of transactions using channels like UPI. The impact is much higher for low-income group customers who are vulnerable and lose money to frauds and scams. The recent proposal by the RBI to compensate up to Rs 25,000 is keeping the small-value frauds in focus. Fraudsters typically exploit customers by either appealing to their greed with promises of high returns or quick profits or by instilling fear through warnings about accounts becoming inactive, delayed KYC processes, or potential digital arrests. As a result, customers unknowingly share OTPs with these fraudsters and lose money. Banks, on the other hand, have been educating customers about frauds and how to protect themselves from becoming victims of such frauds. Ideally, if responsibility can be clearly defined for customers who divulge confidential details or for banks/merchants who fail to implement adequate measures to prevent fraud or educate customers, liability (cost) can be allocated accordingly. In practice, whenever there are incidents, it is difficult to prove the onus, and hence, the RBI has proposed a cost-sharing ratio: 15% to be borne by the customer, 15% by the bank/merchant, and the remaining balance through the pooled industry fund. Possible misuse: Proposed guidelines allow for compensation even if the customer has shared OTP with the fraudster The proposed guidelines allow for compensation even if the customer has shared the OTP with the fraudster. The idea behind this is to protect vulnerable customers and to create a framework of trust in the digital ecosystem. However, such a mechanism can be prone to misuse if customers file for multiple claims for subsequent transactions, especially if they are knowingly sharing details. For preventing misuse, the RBI is considering this as a âone-timeâ compensation per customer. It will be important to see the procedure being laid down to establish the identity of the customer (using mobile number and name de-dupe). Also read: RBI acts on fraud: Customers to get up to Rs 25,000 compensation for losses incurred in small value fraudulent transactions, announces RBI Turnaround time (TAT) for compensation to customers Another important aspect to consider would be the turnaround time (TAT) for compensation. E-payment would be ideal, as the same can be effected immediately once the situation is assessed and customer identity is established, including the veracity of the claim. Consequently, automatic compensation may be avoided. Cost for banks may be impacted The share of liability envisaged currently for a bank may impact the costs, given the volumes being handled. Banks would have to consider robust fraud monitoring systems , and more importantly, prevention-based algorithms to minimise the impact of such one-time claim cases. Artificial intelligence (AI)-based digital fraud detection/prevention systems are already being considered, but it will be imperative to consider the âeffectivenessâ of the same in preventing frauds/scams through the application of rigorous back testing strategies. The author is a Partner at EY Forensic and Integrity ServicesâFinancial Services. Views are personal (Disclaimer: The opinions expressed in this column are that of the writer. The facts and opinions expressed here do not reflect the views of www.economictimes.com .)

headline: The triple blow: Indiaâs gathering economic storm
text: The triple blow: Indiaâs gathering economic storm. India's economy faces significant pressure from global events. Trade protectionism, tighter global money, and an energy crisis in West Asia are creating challenges. These factors are impacting exports, investment, and job creation. Foreign investors are withdrawing capital, affecting stock markets. Rising oil prices are increasing import costs and inflation. A convergence of global shocks is placing the Indian economy under unusual strain. Renewed trade protectionism, tightening global liquidity, and a deepening energy crisis in West Asia are together pushing India towards a prolonged period of macroeconomic stress. The first shock stems from the protectionist turn initiated by President Trump. Elevated tariffs and persistent trade tensions have disrupted global supply chains and dampened export demand. For India, the effects are already visible across key sectorsâfood processing, textiles, pharmaceuticals, engineering goods and industrial intermediatesâwhere exports are stagnating even as costs rise. Prolonged uncertainty has weakened investment sentiment and constrained firms integrated into global value chains. As these employment-intensive sectors slow, the impact is feeding into weaker job creation and rising labour market stress. The second shock is financial, marking the end of an era of ultra-low interest rates in Japan that long channelled cheap capital into emerging markets, including India. For decades, near-zero Japanese rates underpinned abundant global liquidity, enabling investors to borrow cheaply in yen and deploy funds in higher-yielding assets across emerging economies and equity markets. Also Read | Indian economy at risk if Gulf conflict continues: Moody's Analytics That cycle is now reversing. As the Bank of Japan has raised interest rates to around 0.75 per cent, the economics of the âyen carry tradeâ have shifted. Higher borrowing costs and rising currency risks are prompting investors to unwind leveraged positions and repatriate capital. India has felt this acutely, with foreign portfolio investors turning sustained sellers and withdrawing roughly $5â6 billion in the first half of March aloneâone of the sharpest episodes of outflows in recent periods. Equity markets, further pressured by developments in West Asia, have responded accordingly: the Sensex has fallen from its late-2025 peak of around 86,056 to nearly 74,207, while the Nifty has declined from about 26,310 to just above 23,002 as of 19 March 2026, reportedly wiping the wealth of ?34 lakh crore. These corrections are not merely cyclical fluctuations; they reflect a deeper reassessment of risk amid tightening global liquidity. For some time, equity markets have been driven more by liquidity and speculative positioning than by underlying economic fundamentals. The recent correction reflects a partial unwinding of these excesses, alongside a broader tightening of financial conditions. The thirdâand most immediateâshock is the surge in energy prices triggered by the war on Iran and the resulting instability across the Gulf , with immediate and far-reaching consequences. India imports roughly 88 per cent of its crude oil needsâaround 5.5 million barrels per dayâwith an annual import bill of $137 billion at pre-crisis prices in FY 25. Nearly 40% of these imports transit through Hormuz. Oil prices spiked to $120 per barrel, and the risk of further escalation rose, with Iran warning that prices could rise towards $200 per barrel if disruptions in the Gulf deepen. Also Read | Indiaâs limited oil buffers, reliance on subsidies heighten risks from Middle East conflict, Moodyâs warns India imports over 25 million tonnes of liquefied natural gas (LNG) annually, used for power generation, fertiliser production, and as an industrial feedstock, with around 40â45 per cent sourced from Qatar and other Gulf suppliers via the Strait of Hormuz. Dependence is even more pronounced in household energy. Nearly 90 per cent of liquefied petroleum gas (LPG)âwidely used for cooking and small-scale industryâis imported from the Gulf region. This makes household energy costs highly sensitive to supply disruptions and price volatility in West Asia, with immediate implications for inflation and soaring household budgets. Beyond crude, India also imports refined petroleum productsâabout 1.1 million barrels per dayâfrom Gulf suppliers, including diesel, petrol, jet fuel, and naphtha. These dependencies amplify the shock across households, industry and transport. Agriculture is equally exposed: India imports 8â9 million tonnes of urea and 6â7 million tonnes of DAP (around 60 per cent of its requirement), leaving farm output vulnerable to supply disruptions, rising costs and food inflation. External vulnerabilities extend beyond energy. The Gulfâhome to nearly 10 million Indiansâaccounts for about 38 per cent of remittances, roughly $45 billion annually. Any slowdown there could weaken inflows and household consumption. Trade linkages amplify the risk: India exports around $50 billion to the region, with sectors such as gems, food and engineering goods particularly exposed. Disruptions are further amplified by the vulnerability inherent in the United Arab Emirates â role as a regional re-export hub. The danger lies not in any single shock, but in their interaction. Trade disruptions weaken exports just as capital outflows tighten financial conditions. Energy price spikes raise inflation while depressing consumption. External demand softens even as domestic costs rise. Each pressure reinforces the others, creating a feedback loop that could prolong economic stress. Irrespective of the war's duration or outcome, its impact is structural and protracted. Also Read | Indiaâs limited oil buffers, reliance on subsidies heighten risks from Middle East conflict, Moodyâs warns India imports over 25 million tonnes of liquefied natural gas (LNG) annually, used for power generation, fertiliser production, and as an industrial feedstock, with around 40â45 per cent sourced from Qatar and other Gulf suppliers via the Strait of Hormuz. Dependence is even more pronounced in household energy. Nearly 90 per cent of liquefied petroleum gas (LPG)âwidely used for cooking and small-scale industryâis imported from the Gulf region. This makes household energy costs highly sensitive to supply disruptions and price volatility in West Asia, with immediate implications for inflation and soaring household budgets. Beyond crude, India also imports refined petroleum productsâabout 1.1 million barrels per dayâfrom Gulf suppliers, including diesel, petrol, jet fuel, and naphtha. These dependencies amplify the shock across households, industry and transport. Agriculture is equally exposed: India imports 8â9 million tonnes of urea and 6â7 million tonnes of DAP (around 60 per cent of its requirement), leaving farm output vulnerable to supply disruptions, rising costs and food inflation. External vulnerabilities extend beyond energy. The Gulfâhome to nearly 10 million Indiansâaccounts for about 38 per cent of remittances, roughly $45 billion annually. Any slowdown there could weaken inflows and household consumption. Trade linkages amplify the risk: India exports around $50 billion to the region, with sectors such as gems, food and engineering goods particularly exposed. Disruptions are further amplified by the vulnerability inherent in the United Arab Emirates â role as a regional re-export hub. The danger lies not in any single shock, but in their interaction. Trade disruptions weaken exports just as capital outflows tighten financial conditions. Energy price spikes raise inflation while depressing consumption. External demand softens even as domestic costs rise. Each pressure reinforces the others, creating a feedback loop that could prolong economic stress. Irrespective of the war's duration or outcome, its impact is structural and protracted. This is underscored by statements from Qatarâs energy minister that rebuilding gas infrastructureâroughly 17 per cent of which has been damagedâcould cost about $26 billion and take up to five years. Similar timelines are likely to apply to other affected energy assets across the region. More broadly, irrespective of the warâs outcome, the security and economic architecture of Gulf Cooperation Council states is likely to come under renewed scrutiny. Such a scenario will introduce further uncertainty into regional energy markets. Together, these shocks risk stagflationâwhere rising prices coincide with slowing growth and weakening employmentâleaving policymakers caught between inflation control and supporting demand. For policymakers, the task is to prevent these shocks from crystallising into a sustained downturn. This calls for a recalibration of economic, monetary and fiscal strategy. The challenges are manifold: managing inflation without stifling growth, safeguarding external balances through stable trade and capital flows, ensuring energy and fertiliser security, and maintaining adequate liquidity in financial markets. Strengthening supply chain resilience, diversifying energy and trade linkages, accelerating time-bound capital expenditure, and renewing the focus on research and development should be central to the response. For businesses, the imperative is to adapt to a world of higher volatility, fragmented trade and costlier capital. Leveraging FTAs, strengthening intellectual property (IP) capabilities, and upgrading quality and standards will be key to capturing shifting market opportunities. In this new era of overlapping shocks, resilienceânot just growthâwill define economic success. ( Dr. Bhaskar is a former Ambassador and Economist. He is a specialist in West Asia and the author of the book â Indiaâs Energy Security and Economic Development: A Holistic Approach,â published by TERI) (Disclaimer: The opinions expressed in this column are that of the writer. The facts and opinions expressed here do not reflect the views of www.economictimes.com .)

headline: Vedanta shares rise 5% as BofA upgrades stock to Buy, raises target price by 75%. Here’s why

headline: ₹80,000 crore in motor accident claims stuck as compensation system fails victims: Report
text: ₹80,000 crore in motor accident claims stuck as compensation system fails victims: Report. A massive ₹80,000 crore in motor accident compensation claims remains stuck in India's legal system. Many eligible claimants never receive the financial aid they are entitled to, highlighting critical inefficiencies. The country’s most definitive MSME stage returns on March 24 in New Delhi. headline: Samir Arora-backed Helios Flexi Cap Fund adds Tata Motors, exits REC and 2 others
text: Samir Arora-backed Helios Flexi Cap Fund adds Tata Motors, exits REC and 2 others. headline: What's key to India's forex defence? Patra suggests it's $1 trillion buffer
text: What's key to India's forex defence? Patra suggests it's $1 trillion buffer. Former RBI Deputy Governor Michael Patra advocates for India to build foreign exchange reserves to at least $1 trillion. This substantial buffer is crucial for market intervention, covering external debt obligations and cushioning against portfolio investment outflows. headline: RBI working to provide avenues to Russian trade counterparts for INR use
text: RBI working to provide avenues to Russian trade counterparts for INR use. The Reserve Bank of India is creating new ways for Russian businesses to use their accumulated Indian rupees. These rupees can now be used to pay for imports into India or for making investments. This move aims to boost trade in local currencies between India and Russia. Increased transactions will help the market for INR-Rouble trade to grow. Add as a Reliable and Trusted News Source Add Now! (You can now subscribe to our Economic Times WhatsApp channel )

text: No clarity on HDFC Bank chairman’s exit; bank ‘baffled’, RBI backs governance. headline: ED flags Indians buying Dubai properties using credit cards
text: ED flags Indians buying Dubai properties using credit cards. These property buyers had either swiped international credit cards (ICCs) on visits to the Emirates to pay the initial deposit or had clicked on payment links sent by UAE developers-probably, oblivious that they were trampling on Indian laws. Mumbai: When it rains in Dubai, it pours in Mumbai. As the desert metropolis deals with a nervous property market, Indians who used credit cards to buy homes in Dubai are beginning to receive notices from the Enforcement Directorate (ED). These property buyers had either swiped international credit cards (ICCs) on visits to the Emirates to pay the initial deposit or had clicked on payment links sent by UAE developers-probably, oblivious that they were trampling on Indian laws. At least three persons were served notices by the central agency in February, questioning their source of funds for the deals, sources told ET. Credit card transactions are like short-term loans and foreign exchange regulations prohibit individuals from borrowing to buy foreign properties. The Reserve Bank of India (RBI's) liberalised remittance scheme (LTS) used by resident individuals to buy stocks and apartments abroad requires transfer of tax paid funds through official banking channels. Those stunned by the notices and seeking a condonation from ED, as well those who are yet to come under the glare are in a Catch22 situation. They have to bear the hassle and cost of correcting the transaction, paying fine, and in some cases absorbing the loss of selling the property in a weak market with Dubai's reputation as a safe haven under a cloud. "The recent round of ED notices have gone to individuals who may have unknowingly used credit cards to buy UAE properties. They should approach RBI to regularise the mode of payment. RBI may take a lenient view as the money is legitimate even if the mode of payment is wrong. Many a time resident Indians do cross-border purchases without knowing the law or consulting CAs," said Rajesh Shah, partner at the CA firm Jayantilal Thakkar & Co which specialises in forex and anti-money laundering regulations. Prior to applying for compounding -- the task of admitting to violation and settling the contravention by paying a penalty -- the administrative process must be completed. This could in some cases mean reversing the original payment. "The regularisation process could involve remitting fresh funds through banks and instructing the builder to send back the amount that was paid with credit card. In certain circumstances, RBI may ask buyers to sell the property and bring back funds," said Shah. This, however, involves arranging funds (without borrowing) and remitting dollars when rupee is at a new low. If this is unattainable, the buyer may be compelled to sell the property to unwind the transaction. But, bankers feel the regulator may not insist on a reversal as funds were not moved through the hawala network. "Using international credit cards (ICCs) for such purchases falls outside the permitted structure since acquisition of overseas property is treated as a 'capital account' transaction. Where such payments have already been made, individuals may need to examine regularisation options, including the RBI's compounding mechanism," said Moin Ladha, partner at Khaitan & Co. ICCs, like local cards, are meant for current account transactions like buying books, downloading movies, and booking hotels. According to Pankaj Bhuta, founder of the CA firm P. R. Bhuta & Co, even where the ED has initiated investigation against a resident individual, the latter may opt for compounding before the RBI at any time till the conclusion of adjudication proceedings. "Such compounding is typically subject to a no-objection from the ED, which in recent cases has been granted where it is satisfied that permitting compounding is aligned with the true spirit and intent of the Foreign Exchange Management Act (FEMA). In such cases, the RBI is empowered to cap the compounding amount at â¹2 lakhs," said Bhuta. Under LRS a resident individual can transfer up to $250,000 a year to buy assets abroad as well as shop online from India. Some who used ICCs to buy properties perhaps did it to preserve their annual LRS limit. (Join our ETNRI WhatsApp channel for all the latest updates)

headline: ED flags Indians buying Dubai properties using credit cards
text: ED flags Indians buying Dubai properties using credit cards. These property buyers had either swiped international credit cards (ICCs) on visits to the Emirates to pay the initial deposit or had clicked on payment links sent by UAE developers-probably, oblivious that they were trampling on Indian laws. Mumbai: When it rains in Dubai, it pours in Mumbai. As the desert metropolis deals with a nervous property market, Indians who used credit cards to buy homes in Dubai are beginning to receive notices from the Enforcement Directorate (ED). These property buyers had either swiped international credit cards (ICCs) on visits to the Emirates to pay the initial deposit or had clicked on payment links sent by UAE developers-probably, oblivious that they were trampling on Indian laws. At least three persons were served notices by the central agency in February, questioning their source of funds for the deals, sources told ET. Credit card transactions are like short-term loans and foreign exchange regulations prohibit individuals from borrowing to buy foreign properties. The Reserve Bank of India (RBI's) liberalised remittance scheme (LTS) used by resident individuals to buy stocks and apartments abroad requires transfer of tax paid funds through official banking channels. Those stunned by the notices and seeking a condonation from ED, as well those who are yet to come under the glare are in a Catch22 situation. They have to bear the hassle and cost of correcting the transaction, paying fine, and in some cases absorbing the loss of selling the property in a weak market with Dubai's reputation as a safe haven under a cloud. "The recent round of ED notices have gone to individuals who may have unknowingly used credit cards to buy UAE properties. They should approach RBI to regularise the mode of payment. RBI may take a lenient view as the money is legitimate even if the mode of payment is wrong. Many a time resident Indians do cross-border purchases without knowing the law or consulting CAs," said Rajesh Shah, partner at the CA firm Jayantilal Thakkar & Co which specialises in forex and anti-money laundering regulations. Prior to applying for compounding -- the task of admitting to violation and settling the contravention by paying a penalty -- the administrative process must be completed. This could in some cases mean reversing the original payment. "The regularisation process could involve remitting fresh funds through banks and instructing the builder to send back the amount that was paid with credit card. In certain circumstances, RBI may ask buyers to sell the property and bring back funds," said Shah. This, however, involves arranging funds (without borrowing) and remitting dollars when rupee is at a new low. If this is unattainable, the buyer may be compelled to sell the property to unwind the transaction. But, bankers feel the regulator may not insist on a reversal as funds were not moved through the hawala network. "Using international credit cards (ICCs) for such purchases falls outside the permitted structure since acquisition of overseas property is treated as a 'capital account' transaction. Where such payments have already been made, individuals may need to examine regularisation options, including the RBI's compounding mechanism," said Moin Ladha, partner at Khaitan & Co. ICCs, like local cards, are meant for current account transactions like buying books, downloading movies, and booking hotels. According to Pankaj Bhuta, founder of the CA firm P. R. Bhuta & Co, even where the ED has initiated investigation against a resident individual, the latter may opt for compounding before the RBI at any time till the conclusion of adjudication proceedings. "Such compounding is typically subject to a no-objection from the ED, which in recent cases has been granted where it is satisfied that permitting compounding is aligned with the true spirit and intent of the Foreign Exchange Management Act (FEMA). In such cases, the RBI is empowered to cap the compounding amount at â¹2 lakhs," said Bhuta. Under LRS a resident individual can transfer up to $250,000 a year to buy assets abroad as well as shop online from India. Some who used ICCs to buy properties perhaps did it to preserve their annual LRS limit. (Join our ETNRI WhatsApp channel for all the latest updates)

headline: India bank liquidity sees first big deficit of 2026 on tax outflow, lack of RBI support

headline: Two Trades for Today: A power transmission utility for a 7.6% upmove, a large-cap IT stock for a rise of about 5%
text: Two Trades for Today: A power transmission utility for a 7.6% upmove, a large-cap IT stock for a rise of about 5%. The markets suffered one of their worst sessions in recent history as they opened lower and ended with a deep cut on Monday. The Nifty opened with a gap down as it inherited a very weak global trade set-up. It continued to gradually lose ground throughout the day and formed its low point in the late morning session. headline: Nestle India Q3 Results: Profit surges 37% YoY to Rs 908 crore; firm declares Rs 140 per share interim dividend
text: Nestle India Q3 Results: Profit surges 37% YoY to Rs 908 crore; firm declares Rs 140 per share interim dividend. Nestle India reported a 37% increase in net profit for the quarter ending September 30, 2023, reaching Rs 908.1 crore. Revenue from operations also rose by 9.4% YoY to Rs 5,036.82 crore. The company achieved a milestone of crossing Rs 5,000 crore in turnover for the quarter and recorded a double-digit domestic sales growth of 10.3%. Nestle India announced a second interim dividend of Rs 140 per share and approved a stock split. Add as a Reliable and Trusted News Source Add Now!

headline: Svatantra Microfin completes amalgamation with Chaitanya India Fin Credit
text: Svatantra Microfin completes amalgamation with Chaitanya India Fin Credit. headline: Mamaearth IPO to open on October 31, price band announced
text: Mamaearth IPO to open on October 31, price band announced. Honasa Consumer, the company behind personal care brand Mamaearth, is set to launch its initial public offering (IPO) on October 31. The IPO includes fresh shares worth Rs 365 crore and an offer for sale of about 4.12 crore shares. At the upper limit of the price band, the company aims to raise Rs 1,701 crore, valuing it at Rs 10,425 crore. Retail investors have been allocated 10% of the issue size, while qualified institutional buyers have been allocated 75%. Add as a Reliable and Trusted News Source Add Now!

headline: FYI, no great HDFC renunciation his
text: FYI, no great HDFC renunciation his. In a surprising turn of events, HDFC Bank's chairman Atanu Chakraborty has stepped down, citing his personal values as the reason for his departure. This unexpected exit has raised eyebrows among shareholders, leading to a notable drop in the bank's stock prices. However, the Reserve Bank of India reassures that there are no concerns regarding the bankâs management. Prince Siddhartha Gautama renounced worldly life after witnessing specific things: sufferings of old age, sickness and death, and an ascetic who inspired him. Beyond a cryptic statement, 'Certain happenings and practices within the bank that I have observed over last two years are not in congruence with my personal values and ethics,' we are clueless about what caused HDFC Bank chairman Atanu Chakraborty 's 'Great Renunciation'. He certainly has done no service to HDFC Bank. Its shares fell on the news, and the bank's board has had to issue assurances about governance standards and capital buffers. RBI clarified it has no concerns over the management of India's second-largest bank. Chakraborty would have been bound to take up any material issue with the bank's management for corrective action if necessary. He was also free to escalate matters to RBI. Yet, he chose neither course. As HDFC's chairman, he had a responsibility to shareholders that should have been upheld even in his decision to resign. There has been little public airing about contrasting cultures or ethical divergence so far. RBI is a conservative regulator, and it holds systemically important institutions such as HDFC Bank to particularly high standards of governance. The central bank had cleared Chakraborty's appointment as chairman, and will be involved in the leadership transition after his departure. Interim chairman Keki Mistry, who has been with HDFC almost since it was founded, has described Chakraborty's resignation as a 'personal relationship' issue. That is as mystical as banking is material. HDFC Bank is India's most valuable bank by market capitalisation, with extensive FII holdings. This offers the bank's customers and investors comfort over corporate governance and management practices. Individual differences over managerial approach should not be conflated with serious issues of corporate governance. The bank, and its former chairman, should have handled their parting of ways with greater composure. Vague virtue signalling serves no one.

headline: Kotak Mahindra Bank revises debit card fees, ECS/NCH failure charges: updates cash transaction limits
text: Kotak Mahindra Bank revises debit card fees, ECS/NCH failure charges: updates cash transaction limits. Kotak Mahindra Bank is revising several charges for savings and corporate salary accounts, effective April 1, 2026. These include increased ECS/NACH failure fees and a higher annual charge for the Pay Shop More Debit card. Monthly cash transaction limits for certain savings account holders are also being adjusted, alongside the introduction of a new third-party cash transaction fee. Kotak Mahindra Bank has announced revisions in certain charges applicable to savings and corporate salary account holders. The changes cover ECS/NACH failure fees, debit card annual charges, monthly cash transaction limits under banking programmes and the introduction of a third-party cash transaction fee . Income Tax Guide Income Tax Union Budget FY 2026-27 Live Income Tax Slabs FY 2025-26 Income Tax Calculator 2025 Kotak Mahindra Bank ECS/NACH failure fees From April 1, 2026, the first failed debit of ECS (Electronic Clearing Service) and NACH (National Automated Clearing House) will be charged Rs 500 and every subsequent failed debit will be Rs 550. This is applicable to all savings and corporate salary accounts. Currently, Rs 500 is charged for each failed debit. Kotak Mahindra Bank issuance and annual maintenance fee for Pay Shop More Debit card For its Pay Shop More Debit card, Kotak Mahindra Bank will levy a charge of Rs 285 per annum, which will be an increase from the earlier fee of Rs 259 per annum. Revision in monthly cash transaction limits This revision is applicable only for savings account holders under Kotak banking programmes#. For customers who do not fall within the prescribed *Relationship Value (RV) criteria under their respective program (Privy, Privy+, Neon, Platinum, Maxima, Black, or Solitaire), the following limits will apply: A maximum of 3 cash transactions per month, and A combined monthly cash transaction value of up to Rs 1,00,000 If you exceed these limits, charges will apply as per the applicable schedule of charges. #Programmes Covered: Kotak Solitaire, Privy, Privy+, Private, Neon, Maxima, Platinum and Black. If you are part of any of these programmes, programme benefits will apply first, even if your account has other product-level benefits. For complete details, please refer to the programme-level GSFC . What does it mean for Kotak Mahindra Bank account holders? The bank has explained with an example: If you are enrolled in the Kotak Premium Banking Programme requiring an RV of Rs 75 lakh: âFor May 2026, your maintained RV is Rs 60 lakh, which is below the required threshold. During May, you continue to receive programme benefits. After the month-end review, since the RV requirement was below required criteria: You become eligible for 3 free cash transactions and a Rs 1 lakh monthly cash limit for May 2026 transactions. Only the incremental difference (over and above what was already recovered during the month) will be levied. Note that GST will be charged on this incremental amount. Introduction of third-party cash transaction fee Under the newly introduced third-party cash transaction fee structure, customers will get a monthly free limit of Rs 25,000. Any amount exceeding this limit will attract a charge of Rs 5 per Rs1,000, subject to a minimum fee of Rs 150 per transaction.

headline: Geopolitical heat lifts inflation fears but RBI's repo rate hike unlikely
text: Geopolitical heat lifts inflation fears but RBI's repo rate hike unlikely. RBI MPC member Saugata Bhattacharya says a rate hike is “negligible” despite inflation risks from crude, metals and weather. He sees no economic overheating, notes improving credit growth and steady consumption, and expects inflation to near 4%. He adds new GDP and CPI series will better reflect India’s economy. headline: Manappuram Finance shares drop 2% after RBI gives nod for Rs 4,385 crore Bain Capital deal
text: Manappuram Finance shares drop 2% after RBI gives nod for Rs 4,385 crore Bain Capital deal. Manappuram Finance shares dipped 2% after its subsidiaries received RBI approval for Bain Capital's indirect control, marking the final step in the Rs 4,385 crore deal. Bain Capital will hold a significant stake, becoming a joint promoter alongside existing ones, with the capital infusion expected by March 31, 2026. Add as a Reliable and Trusted News Source Add Now! headline: ATM error took Rs 10,000 from Surat man. 9 years later, that glitch won him Rs 3.28 lakh
text: ATM error took Rs 10,000 from Surat man. 9 years later, that glitch won him Rs 3.28 lakh. A consumer court in Surat has ordered Bank of Baroda to pay over Rs 3 lakh to a customer after it failed to refund Rs 10,000 from a failed ATM withdrawal for nearly nine years. The customer pursued the matter for several years. The court cited RBI rules on timely refunds, and the ruling highlights the importance of bank accountability in handling customer complaints. headline: Buy in staggered manner amid global uncertainty: Go domestic, bet on IT and realty, says Unmesh Sharma
text: Buy in staggered manner amid global uncertainty: Go domestic, bet on IT and realty, says Unmesh Sharma. Indian equity markets present a cautious yet opportunistic scenario, with valuations now below their long-term average. HDFC Securities' Unmesh Sharma outlines a three-scenario outlook on global energy disruptions, suggesting staggered buying and a domestic focus. headline: Government introduces bill to amend companies, LLP laws; proposes changes in CSR norms
text: Government introduces bill to amend companies, LLP laws; proposes changes in CSR norms. The government has proposed significant amendments to company and LLP laws to boost ease of doing business. Key changes include decriminalizing procedural defaults, relaxing CSR norms, allowing hybrid AGMs, and simplifying M&A procedures. These reforms aim to reduce compliance burdens for businesses, especially small firms and startups, and align India's corporate framework with global standards. headline: Indian lenders pay steepest premium for short-term funds in 6 years as borrowings hit record
text: Indian lenders pay steepest premium for short-term funds in 6 years as borrowings hit record. Indian banks are paying record premiums for short-term funds. This is driven by sluggish deposit growth and a heavy reliance on certificates of deposit. Borrowings have reached an all-time high. Experts anticipate this pressure to continue until the end of the year. Banks are seeking measures to ease this funding strain. headline: Coal India board approves up to 35% divestment in SECL, Mahanadi Coalfields
text: Coal India board approves up to 35% divestment in SECL, Mahanadi Coalfields. Coal India's board has approved divesting up to 25% of its stake in South Eastern Coalfields Ltd (SECL) via Offer for Sale, alongside a fresh issuance of up to 10% equity through an IPO or other market routes. This move, along with a similar divestment in Mahanadi Coalfields Limited, aims to raise capital and explore overseas opportunities in critical minerals. headline: RBI's likely to raise liquidity to keep 'short' rates in check
text: RBI's likely to raise liquidity to keep 'short' rates in check. The Reserve Bank of India is expected to increase money supply soon. This move aims to prevent short-term interest rates from rising sharply. It will also counter the effects of the RBI's efforts to support the Rupee. headline: Zomato’s fee hike to boost margins, demand still intact : Jignanshu Gor
text: Zomato’s fee hike to boost margins, demand still intact : Jignanshu Gor. Zomato has increased its platform fee, aiming for better profits. This move puts it ahead of rival Swiggy in charges. headline: Credit guarantee scheme unlikely to benefit lower rated MFIs
text: Credit guarantee scheme unlikely to benefit lower rated MFIs. A new credit guarantee scheme aims to boost lending to microfinance firms. However, banks remain cautious. headline: RBI's March-end rupee support faces test from war and oil surge this year
text: RBI's March-end rupee support faces test from war and oil surge this year. Historically, the rupee strengthens by March 31 due to RBI intervention, benefiting corporate balance sheets. However, this year, elevated oil prices and geopolitical tensions have led to significant RBI dollar sales, potentially challenging this trend. Traders anticipate a close around 91.75-92.50 per dollar, with a weaker outcome if interventions decrease amid sustained high oil prices. Stronger intervention likely, local unit expected to close near 92 on March 31 Add as a Reliable and Trusted News Source Add Now! (You can now subscribe to our ETMarkets WhatsApp channel )

text: Kotak Mahindra Bank arm KMCC to sell part stake in Infina for Rs 1,294 crore. Kotak Mahindra Capital Company will sell a 30.99% stake in Infina Finance for over ₹12,939 crore. headline: RBI's bond purchases jump after Iran war
text: RBI's bond purchases jump after Iran war. India's central bank  has boosted its secondary-market  government bond purchases significantly since the Iran  war broke out, data showed on Friday. Bonds Corner Powered By Bonds log biggest selloff in 2-1/2 years on higher oil, US yields Indian government bonds plunged on  Monday, as elevated oil prices  and rising U.S. Treasury yields triggered the sharpest selloff since  October 2023, although bonds recouped half of their losses after positive commentary from U.S. President Trump on Iran war. headline: Govt may consider OFS option for raising public float in IDBI Bank
text: Govt may consider OFS option for raising public float in IDBI Bank. The government is considering an Offer-for-Sale (OFS) in IDBI Bank to raise public shareholding after a failed attempt to sell a majority stake. The move aims to improve valuation and price discovery, as the current public float is just 5.29%. The country’s most definitive MSME stage returns on March 24 in New Delhi. headline: HDFC Bank: What exactly was it? Atanu Chakrabarty's shock exit baffles global investors
text: HDFC Bank: What exactly was it? Atanu Chakrabarty's shock exit baffles global investors. HDFC Bank's stock plunged after chairman Atanu Chakraborty resigned citing ethical concerns. Investors expressed disbelief during a call where management offered no clear explanations. Chakraborty, a former bureaucrat, later called his departure 'routine,' but the market reacted sharply, wiping billions in value. Analysts questioned the board's lack of specifics regarding the resignation. The country’s most definitive MSME stage returns on March 24 in New Delhi.
